meta.titre: At the Root — Arabic through its structure
meta.source_principale: Maurice Gloton, Approche du Coran par la grammaire et le lexique (Albouraq, 2002)

r-h-m.field: Organic bond, enveloping gentleness, indulgence, protection. The root first denotes the womb (al-raḥim) — the absolute prototype of the bond: that which contains, nourishes and shelters before any word is spoken.
r-h-m.glose_gloton: Gloton describes this root as the idea of a dilatable space where a seed is laid down and, through a process of love, brings latent possibilities into being — whence the notions of womb, of mercy, of expansive love, of flowering, radiance and germination. The central verb — to radiate love, to take pity, to show mercy, to feel compassion, to be clement — carries, in his reading, a striking further nuance: to die in the wake of childbirth. Divine mercy is therefore, for Gloton, this dilatable place where the seed comes into being: a love that radiates by germinating, and that may cost the one who carries it.
r-h-m.meditation: What matrixial mercy is carrying me at this very moment, without my knowing it?
forms: to show mercy, to take into compassion
to cover with mercy, to pray for someone's mercy
to show mercy to one another
the womb, the matrixial bond, the bond of kinship
quran: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Most Merciful.
And My mercy embraces all things.
And We have sent you only as a mercy for the worlds.

ʿ-l-m.core_fr: The knowing that reads the signs
ʿ-l-m.field: To know by sign. The root shares with ʿalāma (the landmark, the boundary marker) and ʿalam (the banner, the flag) one and the same intuition: to know is to perceive a mark that sets one thing apart from another. To know God is to perceive His signs (āyāt) in the world.
ʿ-l-m.glose_gloton: Gloton traces a remarkable genesis of knowledge here: first to mark, to distinguish by a mark or a sign, to fix, to cleave; then to discern, to signify, to establish a link between different signs — to know, to be learned, to tell one thing from another, to be instructed, informed, aware of, to surpass another in knowledge. Knowledge, in Arabic, is thus born of the gesture of marking — of cleaving matter so as to set a distinguishing sign upon it. The noun ʿalāma names the distinctive mark that separates two or more things: any landmark, clue, mountain, standard, flag, boundary stone, marker of limits, milestone, banner or border. To know, in this intuition, is to establish a link between signs; and the universe (ʿālam) is itself, etymologically, the signifier.
ʿ-l-m.meditation: What signs am I passing through without seeing — even as they signal to me?
forms: the One who knows absolutely everything
the mark, the sign, the landmark
the master, the one who teaches
quran: He who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know.
And He taught Adam all the names.

q-r-b.core_fr: The night journey toward the near
q-r-b.field: To be near, to draw close. The root describes at once the distance that shrinks and the quality of intimacy. The word qurbān (sacrifice) comes from it: that which brings one closer to the divine.
q-r-b.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete, almost biblical scene: to walk through the night until daybreak, to make a journey by night, to find oneself right beside someone, to draw near, to be in proximity, to be close, imminent, to come near, to border on, to gain access, to become easy — to return the sabre to its scabbard. To approach, in Arabic, is to walk by night. And qurb covers a whole field: nearness, neighbourhood, drawing-together, kinship, consanguinity, familiarity, the means of drawing closer, trust, credit, the favour one enjoys in another's presence. More subtle still — Gloton's definition explicitly names a practice of worship that fosters nearness to God, a means of approach, good works. The root itself, then, already carries its spiritual dimension.
q-r-b.meditation: He is nearer to me than my jugular vein — where do I stand in relation to Him at this moment?
forms: to bring near, to make approach
to seek to draw closer (to God)
the sacrificial offering (that which brings near)
quran: And We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.
And when My servants ask you about Me — I am near.

dh-k-r.field: To mention, to remember, to call to mind. The root holds together three inseparable acts: to recall inwardly, to name with the tongue, and to make present what is remembered. The Sufi ذِكْر is this trinity.
dh-k-r.glose_gloton: Gloton uncovers here a startling kinship that ordinary translation veils: one and the same root names the organ of generation — the male, who emits the seed — and the act of remembering. As he sets it out: to touch, strike or wound someone at the organ of generation; to remember, to recall, to mention, to bring back to mind. The dhikr, in this light, stands close to the gesture that fertilises: to remember God is to let a seed fall that takes root and grows. Remembrance and the male share their root because both deposit.
dh-k-r.meditation: When I speak His Name — am I speaking, or am I remembering?
forms: to recall, to call back to mind
to take heed, to remember deeply
the remembrance, the invocation, the Quran itself
the memory, the act of recollection
that which serves to remind, the teaching reminder
the one who remembers and invokes
quran: Remember Me, and I shall remember you.
Is it not by the remembrance of God that hearts find rest?

ʿ-b-d.field: To serve, to worship. The root denotes at once the condition (to be a servant) and the act (to worship). In Arabic, ʿabd carries no connotation of humiliation: it is the noblest of titles — Muḥammad is called first ʿabd, servant, before being called rasūl, messenger.
ʿ-b-d.glose_gloton: For this root Gloton sets out a sweep of verbs of service that covers the full breadth of the sense: to serve, to be in service, to work, to make an effort, to strive, to grow pious, to give oneself wholly, to worship, to render a cult, to have devotion for, to venerate, to hold an office, to act as deputy. The ʿabd is, in his definition, the one who serves, the servant, the worshipper, the holder of a humble office, the bondsman — the servant or worshipper of God. And the abstract noun ʿibāda gathers together service, servitude, worship, humble office, devotion and cult. For Gloton, then, the boundary between work, office and worship dissolves within this root: the one who truly serves worships, whether he knows it or not.
ʿ-b-d.meditation: Am I the servant of what I believe I serve — or of what I serve in secret?
forms: to devote oneself to service, to perform works of devotion
to enslave, to take as a servant
the servant (a noble title in Arabic)
the quality of servanthood, the condition of service
the One who is worshipped, the Adored
quran: I created the jinn and humankind only that they might serve Me.
It is You we serve, and it is You whose help we implore.

q-l-b.core_fr: The heart — an organ in perpetual movement
q-l-b.core_fr_original: The heart, that which turns over
q-l-b.field: To turn, to overturn, to change. The heart (al-qalb) is so named because it ceaselessly turns over — from one state to another, from one affection to another. The root shares with inqilāb (overturning) and taqallub (oscillation) a single intuition: that which turns.
q-l-b.glose_gloton: Gloton gives a striking definition of the heart through this root: to turn, to turn over, to tumble, to dig, to invert, to interchange, to permute, to mutate, to overturn, to upset, to tip over, to jostle, to set something the wrong way round, to draw the pith from the palm, to strike someone in the heart; and, conversely, to be turned over, to be overturned. The verbal noun qalb covers conversion, inversion, permutation, overturning, reversal, change, sudden turn, upheaval. And on the same line he adds: heart — the central organ of physical, psychological and spiritual life, in perpetual mutation or movement — spirit, soul, inward being, centre, pith, kernel, essence. For Gloton, then, the Arabic heart is by definition the organ that turns over: not a stable seat of identity, but the site of continual reversal. The word and the organ share their instability.
q-l-b.meditation: My heart, at this moment — toward which side has it turned?
forms: to turn over and over, to examine from every angle
to oscillate, to vary, to change state
to be turned over, to be overturned
the heart, that which turns over
the one who turns, who returns
quran: And know that God comes between a man and his heart.
Save the one who comes to God with a sound heart.

n-f-s.core_fr: The precious breath — soul made flesh
n-f-s.core_fr_original: The soul, the breath, the person
n-f-s.field: To breathe, to breathe out, to exhale. The same root yields nafs (the soul, the person) and nafas (the breath). In Arabic, what animates a being is what breathes it. The nafs is therefore not an abstract “self” — it is a being that breathes.
n-f-s.glose_gloton: For this root Gloton unfolds a surprising range: to have an influence on someone through breath or gaze, to recover from childbirth, to regret what one gives away, to be precious, of great price, to be sought after. The noun nafs he defines as the (embodied) soul, breath, animating breath, vital principle, blood, essence, being, individual, the very depth of a thing, one's own person, oneself, the eye, the gaze. And the derived forms: to breathe, to heave a sigh, to exhale, to open out, to be relieved, to shine. For Gloton, then, the nafs is neither an abstract self nor a breath set apart: it is the embodied-being-that-breathes, and — a striking fact — it shares its root with nafīs (precious). The soul is precious because it is what can be regretted once given away; and the gift of self is, by its very root, a gift of the precious.
n-f-s.meditation: This breath I have just drawn — did I truly feel it, or has it already passed without me?
forms: to relieve, to let breathe, to undo what stifles
the soul, the person, the self
the precious, that which is worth a soul
quran: By a soul and the One who shaped it — He breathed into it both its waywardness and its piety; blessed is the one who purifies it.
We created man, and We know what his soul whispers to him.

r-w-h.field: To breathe, to vivify, to give rest. The root r-w-ḥ unites three fields: rīḥ (the wind), rūḥ (the spirit), rāḥa (rest). All three speak a single airy movement — that which circulates, vivifies, soothes.
r-w-h.glose_gloton: Beneath this root Gloton finds several inseparable nuances: the idea of a sweet fragrance, of a movement of air that cools and brings rest in the evening, to do something at evening, to set out at evening, to set (of the sun), to catch wind of something, to be exposed to the wind, to rejoice in something. The first derivative, rawḥ, is the cool light breath of the wind — rest, joy, ease, contentment, compassion, assistance, succour; and only at the next degree comes rūḥ — the spiritual breath of life, the vital spirit, the spiritual principle of life, the soul, the divine Spirit. The spirit, in Quranic Arabic, is therefore not cut off from the evening wind: it is the cooling air itself, raised to the point of bearing life.
r-w-h.meditation: This breathing that passes through me — does it come from me, or am I within it?
forms: to go, to set out at evening; to find rest
the aromatic plant, the basil of paradise
the breeze, the light breath, mercy
quran: They ask you about the Spirit. Say: the Spirit is of the command of my Lord.
When I have shaped him in harmony and breathed into him of My Spirit.

h-b-b.core_fr_original: Love, the seed of the heart
h-b-b.field: To love, to take root, to germinate. The root ḥ-b-b unites love (ḥubb) and the seed (ḥabba): to love, in Arabic, is to let a seed implant itself and grow. Love is not an emotion — it is a plant taking hold.
h-b-b.glose_gloton: Gloton is explicit about the bond between the seed and love, which he sets down as a central intuition: the idea of a grain, of a seed within which love is enclosed — creation being brought forth by and within Love. The verbs — to love, to cherish, to feel affection for, to will — descend from this seminal root. The noun ḥabba is the grain, the seed, at once a collective and a unit noun; the verbal noun ḥubb is love, affection, fertilising, germinating love. For Gloton, to love is to let a seed take hold — not to undergo a passion that passes.
h-b-b.meditation: The love I carry — is it a seed that grows, or a fire that burns me without lasting?
forms: to make dear, to make beloved
quran: He loves them and they love Him.
Say: if you love God, follow me — God will love you.

s-b-r.field: To bind fast, to hold back, to bear. The root ṣ-b-r is concrete: ṣabbara is to salt a thing in order to preserve it, to hold it. Arabic patience is not passivity — it is a firm holding through time, against all that would scatter.
s-b-r.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete act before reaching its moral sense: first to bind, to fasten someone to something, to hold a person back so as to prevent them from acting, to restrain, to hinder, to force, to compel; then to be constant, patient, to wait, to bear with endurance, to endure. Patience is therefore first a bond: to hold oneself fastened to what does not change, there where everything urges flight. It is no passive virtue — it is an active firmness, a knot pulled tight against the wind.
s-b-r.meditation: When I believe I am being patient — am I holding, or am I merely enduring?
forms: the One whose patience never wearies (Divine Name)
quran: O you who believe, seek help in patience and prayer — God is with the patient.
The patient will be rewarded without measure.

sh-k-r.core_fr_original: Gratitude, the thankfulness that gives back
sh-k-r.field: To acknowledge, to give thanks, to make fruitful. The root originally describes the growth of a well-fed animal: shakara is to make fruitful what one has received. Arabic gratitude is not an inward sentiment — it is the act of making the gift grow.
sh-k-r.glose_gloton: Gloton ties the idea of gratitude to that of organic increase: the idea of growth, of fruitfulness, of abundance, of yield, of fullness, of acknowledgement. The verbs — to thank, to praise, to be grateful, to render thanks, to reward — stand, surprisingly, alongside concrete images: to produce offspring, to give milk abundantly, to be heavy with rain, to grow generous. To give thanks, within this root, is to let the gift bear fruit — as a well-fed beast grows sleek, as a rain-laden cloud fertilises the earth.
sh-k-r.meditation: The gift I received yesterday — did I make it bear fruit, or did I let it wither?
forms: the One who acknowledges and rewards generously (Divine Name)
that for which thanks are given, that which is rewarded
quran: If you are grateful, I shall increase you.
Be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful toward Me.

n-w-r.core_fr: The light that reveals and that we flee
n-w-r.field: To shine, to illumine, to flower. The root n-w-r unites light (nūr), fire (nār) and flower (nawr): three manifestations of a single phenomenon — that which comes out of itself to become visible.
n-w-r.glose_gloton: Gloton renders the twofold movement of this root: to shine, to glow, to glimpse a fire or a light from afar, to illumine, to radiate, to give light — and also to avoid something, to take flight, to flee. Strikingly, one and the same root carries to illumine and to flee — as though light, when sought, both lit the way and drove one back. Quranic light is no mere lighting: it is a presence that reveals, yet one the creature sometimes flees as much as it is drawn to it.
n-w-r.meditation: The light that touches me at this moment — where does it come from? And what does it illumine in me?
forms: to light up, to illumine abundantly
to seek the light, to be illumined
the Luminous, the One who gives light
quran: God is the Light of the heavens and the earth.
A light has come to you from God, and a clear Book.

h-q-q.core_fr: That which imposes itself as real
h-q-q.core_fr_original: The real, the true, the due
h-q-q.field: To be established, founded, just. The root ḥ-q-q unites three senses: what is (the real), what ought to be (the just, the right), and what is owed (the obligation). The True is God — al-Ḥaqq.
h-q-q.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a legal intuition before reaching the metaphysical: to prevail over someone by right, to be obliged, to render necessary, indispensable, to be owed, to know with certainty, to make sure of, to fall exactly right, to be ascertained, to be realised. The ḥaqq, in this light, is no abstract truth — it is that which imposes itself, that which cannot but be. To say that God is al-Ḥaqq is to say that He is what cannot not be: He alone falls exactly right, He alone is ascertained, He alone is realised. The derived noun ḥaqīqa therefore names essential reality: that which remains when all else has passed.
h-q-q.meditation: What I hold to be real — is it truly real, or does it merely last a little longer than the rest?
forms: to be true, to be established
to realise, to bring truly into being
to be realised, to be verified
to deserve, to have a right to
the one who is in the right
quran: That is because God, He is the True.
Until it becomes clear to them that He is the True.

f-q-r.core_fr: The broken spine that no longer stands alone
f-q-r.field: To have a broken back, to be in need. The root f-q-r evokes, concretely, the broken vertebra — the one who can no longer stand upright by himself. Sufi poverty is not material privation: it is the awareness of being held.
f-q-r.glose_gloton: Gloton describes this root in its most striking concreteness: to dig the earth, to pierce, to perforate, to strip bare, to break the vertebrae, to break a person, to crush them, to be in need, in destitution, in scarcity, to be deprived, poor. Arabic poverty is no mere want: it is, etymologically, a broken spine — the one who can no longer hold himself upright by his own strength. The noun faqr covers poverty, need, indigence, the state of being stripped bare, lack, misery, care, sorrow, grief. And the faqīr is the one stripped bare, poor, lacking, indigent, needy, owning nothing, destitute. For Gloton, then, Sufi faqr is not metaphor but observation: the creature is, by its very structure, broken at the spine — it stands upright only because it is held.
f-q-r.meditation: Am I aware, at this instant, that I do not stand upright by my own strength?
forms: to break the spine, to impoverish
to make poor, to put in need
to be in need, to lack essentially
essential poverty, the need for God
the poor one, the one who has need
the felt sense of essential need
quran: O humankind, it is you who are poor and in need of God — and God is the Rich, the Worthy of praise.
God is the Rich, and it is you who are the poor.

t-w-b.core_fr: The return that does not cease
t-w-b.field: To return, to turn back. The root t-w-b describes a return — that of the human being who had strayed, but also that of God who turns back toward His creature. The movement runs both ways: tāba may take God as its subject (He turns toward us) or the human being (he returns to God).
t-w-b.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root four verbs in a chain: to return, to turn back, to convert, to repent. The verbal noun — tawba — means return, repentance, conversion, and the intensive noun tawwāb the one who returns unceasingly. Unceasingly — the word matters: for Gloton, tawba is not a single act but a quality of the heart that turns back with every breath. And God, like the human being, is named by this root: He is at-Tawwāb, the One who returns ever toward His creature.
t-w-b.meditation: God has already turned back toward me — shall I, in turn, take a step toward Him?
forms: to return (on the part of the human being); to accept repentance (on the part of God)
to call to return, to urge to repentance
the one who returns, the penitent
the One who ever returns, who ever accepts the return (Divine Name)
quran: Then He turned back toward them, that they might turn back.
God loves those who return unceasingly.

ʾ-l-h.core_fr: To worship — to be struck with bewilderment
ʾ-l-h.core_fr_original: The worshipped God, that toward which the heart turns
ʾ-l-h.field: That which is worshipped, that toward which a being turns to entrust itself. Allāh is the contraction of al-ilāh — the God, the Worshipped par excellence. The root shares with walaha (to be lost in love) the whole field of the distraught.
ʾ-l-h.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — the most central in all Quranic Arabic — a disconcerting unfolding: the root that forms the names Allāh and Ilāh — to worship as God (or a god) — to be in bewilderment — to take refuge, to flee for safety — to protect. The word Ilāh covers all that is worshipped: the term applies as much to God manifesting Himself as to entities one serves or worships. And Gloton notes — a remarkable philological point — that the form al-Ilāh (The God) is found neither in the Qurʾān nor in the Sunna: Allāh is, in Arabic, a proper name, not a determined common noun. But the idea of worship within the root is inseparable from bewilderment: to worship, etymologically, is to be struck with astonishment.
ʾ-l-h.meditation: Toward what does my heart truly turn, when I believe I am praying?
forms: to worship, to render a cult
to make oneself a god, to claim divinity
quran: And your God is one God — there is no god but He, the All-Merciful, the Most Merciful.
When it was said to them: there is no god but God, they swelled with pride.
Say: He, God, is One (Aḥad).

ʾ-ḥ-d.field: Aḥad is not a unity one could count; it is absolute uniqueness, with no possible second. Wāḥid may open a series, aḥad excludes any series. It is used negatively (“no one”) and positively (“the Sole”).
ʾ-ḥ-d.glose_gloton: Gloton carefully distinguishes this root from its cousin w-ḥ-d. If w-ḥ-d speaks simple unity, ʾ-ḥ-d speaks exclusive uniqueness: to be unique, sole, solitary, singular, without peer, isolated, separate, identical to. The noun is defined as the state of being unique — unique, sole, singular, solitary, solitude, one, isolated — uniqueness. It is this word, aḥad, that closes the Quranic profession of faith of sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ: Allāh is Aḥad — Allāh is the Unique-without-peer. For Gloton the difference from wāḥid is cardinal: wāḥid is one among other possible ones (numerical); aḥad is sole, with no other even thinkable beside it (ontological). It is this uniqueness that the Sufis confess in the tawḥīd.
ʾ-ḥ-d.meditation: Is there in me a silent “second” to whom I grant a place — beside Him?
forms: essential uniqueness (Sufi term: God in Himself)
quran: Say: He, God, is One. God, the absolute Recourse. He did not beget, He was not begotten, and none is equal to Him.
The places of prostration belong to God: call upon no one alongside God.

w-ḥ-d.core_fr: Simple unity — to be one with oneself
w-ḥ-d.core_fr_original: To make one, to recognise the One
w-ḥ-d.field: To be one, to set apart, to make one. The root describes both the state (wāḥid) and the act (tawḥīd). To recognise unity is not to formulate a thesis — it is to unify one's gaze, one's intention, one's heart, until one sees nothing but Him in all things.
w-ḥ-d.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root, distinct from ʾ-ḥ-d, a precise core: to be alone, unique, isolated, separate, simple, uncompounded, to set oneself apart, to occupy oneself alone with something. The noun waḥda covers unity, one, individual, person, one of a pair or couple, sole, unique, having no peer, odd-numbered, simple, isolated, particular. The verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān XXXIV, 46) is illuminating: God exhorts the believers to rise in pairs or singly to meditate. Meditation may therefore be collective or solitary — uniqueness (waḥda) is not sorrowful solitude but the recollectedness in which one is one with oneself.
w-ḥ-d.meditation: Where, within me, has unity split apart today — and what would it take to mend it?
forms: to be unique, to be isolated
to unify, to recognise the unity of God
to become unified, to become one within oneself
to unite with, to become one with
the attesting and realising of divine uniqueness
quran: And when God is mentioned, He alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the hereafter shrink back.
And your God is one God.

ṣ-m-d.core_fr: The absolute Recourse, the Full without hollow
ṣ-m-d.field: That which is full, massive, without interstice — and that toward which one turns for support. The two senses hold together: only what is full can be a flawless support. Aṣ-Ṣamad needs nothing, and everything depends on Him.
ṣ-m-d.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of remarkable metaphysical density: the idea of a solid support, or of a precise goal toward which one sets out — to head toward someone, to go to someone's dwelling, to set, to place, to fix something; to lean upon someone, to hold firm, to resist, to stand one's ground, to need nothing in order to exist. And the noun he defines explicitly as a divine name — the One upon whom one leans — the Impenetrable — the universal Support, master, lord, the one to whom all turns and who orders every affair — massive, having neither hollow nor protrusion. Gloton cites sūra 112 directly: He, Allāh, is One — Allāh, the Impenetrable universal Support. For Gloton, then, the Quranic Ṣamad is all at once: the goal toward which one heads, the support upon which one leans, and the being without hollow — wholly full, without lack, without need. He is the one absolute recourse.
ṣ-m-d.meditation: Toward what do I turn when I am in want — and is it full enough to carry me?
forms: to turn toward, to aim at, to have recourse to
quran: God, the absolute Recourse.

q-d-s.field: To sanctify, to purify, to set apart. The holy is that which is set apart, not out of contempt but out of transcendence. Al-Quds = Jerusalem, the holy city; Rūḥ al-Qudus = the holy Breath that sanctifies.
q-d-s.glose_gloton: Gloton draws this root tight around its radiance: to be pure, spotless, without mixture, immaculate, holy. The active verbal form (qaddasa) means to purify, to sanctify, to proclaim holiness, to make holy, to consecrate, to hallow. And he notes explicitly that al-Quddūs, the Most Holy, is one of the Names of God. The verse he cites (Qurʾān II, 87) speaks of Jesus strengthened by the Spirit of Holiness — rūḥ al-qudus. Arabic holiness is therefore not a superior moral quality: it is the absence of mixture, radical limpidity, that which has no veil upon itself. To sanctify something is to remove from it what clouds it.
q-d-s.meditation: What, within me, asks to be set apart — not out of contempt for the rest, but to make room for Him?
forms: to be sanctified, to purify oneself
the holy Breath, the Spirit of holiness
sanctification, the act of proclaiming holiness
quran: He is God — there is no god but He — the King, the Most Holy.
And we glorify You with Your praise and sanctify You.

s-b-ḥ.core_fr: To glorify by transcending — “he swims far from all imperfection”
s-b-ḥ.field: The primary verb sabaḥa = to swim, to traverse a great space. From it, sabbaḥa: to glorify by setting God far from all imperfection — as a swimmer moves away from the shore. Subḥān Allāh is this movement, not a pious cry.
s-b-ḥ.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking intuition: to swim, to bathe, to move as though swimming, to glide along, to walk swiftly, to exalt someone, to be free of every flaw. Arabic glorification (tasbīḥ) is therefore, etymologically, a swimming movement: one glides far from all that would lower God. To say subḥān Allāh is not merely “glory to God” — it is “I swim far from any imperfection I might attribute to Him.” The classical tanzīh (transcendence) of Sufism finds its source here: to transcend is to make God swim clear of human images.
s-b-ḥ.meditation: What image of Him have I let settle in, that needs to be set far off once again?
forms: to swim, to float, to traverse
quran: The seven heavens glorify Him, and the earth, and all who are within them — there is no thing that does not glorify Him with His praise.
And each one swims in an orbit.

ʾ-m-n.core_fr: Faith — to place oneself in safety within
ʾ-m-n.core_fr_original: Faith — to be in safety, to entrust oneself
ʾ-m-n.field: To be in safety, to trust, to be worthy of trust. Faith (īmān) is not first of all assent to dogmas — it is the state of a heart at safety because it has entrusted itself. The amāna is what is confided to one worthy of it.
ʾ-m-n.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root — the root of faith (īmān) — a core of great limpidity: the idea of safety, security, trust, safekeeping, of being assured, of adhering, of fidelity, of giving credence, of loyalty, of protection, of belief. For Gloton, īmān is therefore not to believe that — in the sense in which belief is taken as intellectual assent to a proposition. It is to place oneself in safety within, to adhere to, to trust in. To believe, in Quranic Arabic, is to lay oneself down. And a remarkable fact: the same root gives amīn (trustworthy, sure) and the word āmīn that all the Abrahamic religions pronounce at the close of prayer — the so be it which is, etymologically, an I place myself in safety within what has just been said.
ʾ-m-n.meditation: Is my heart at safety because it knows — or because it has entrusted itself?
forms: to be in safety, to be sheltered
to seek protection, to place oneself in someone's keeping
the deposit, that which is confided to me
the believer, the trusting one; al-Muʾmin = God, the source of safety
amen — so be it, in trust
quran: Those who have believed and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of God — is it not by the remembrance of God that hearts find rest?
We offered the deposit to the heavens, the earth and the mountains — they refused to bear it, they took fright at it; and the human being bore it.

s-l-m.core_fr: To give oneself up in order to be saved
s-l-m.core_fr_original: Peace — the wholeness of what entrusts itself entire
s-l-m.field: A single root for peace, salvation, wholeness, surrender. Salima: to be whole. Aslama: to hand over. Salām: the peace that results. The deep link: only what gives itself entire stays entire.
s-l-m.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this decisive root onto an etymological paradox: first to sting someone (a serpent, a chastisement), to inflict a bite — then to be safe and sound, to remain intact, to keep in good condition, to fare well, to escape, to be exempt from. One and the same root therefore carries the serpent's bite and the state of being safe and sound — as though the root itself bore, in its ambivalence, the twofold movement of threat and salvation. The noun salām (peace) covers peace, the peaceable, the tranquil, one who loves to live in safety, obedience, islam. And the word islām Gloton defines: the act of giving oneself up safe and sound, a captive who has surrendered in order to keep his life. Islam, etymologically, is to give oneself up in order to be saved.
s-l-m.meditation: What am I still holding back, that I might hand over in order to become whole again?
forms: to be whole, to be unharmed, to be at peace
to greet, to deliver, to hand over in good condition
to hand oneself over, to commit oneself to God
to surrender, to yield in trust
the giving of oneself to God
the one who has given himself over
whole, unharmed (the whole heart, qalb salīm)
quran: Save the one who comes to God with a whole heart.
O you who have believed, enter into peace all together.
the King, the Most Holy, the Peace, the Granter of safety.

sh-h-d.core_fr: To be present to what one sees
sh-h-d.core_fr_original: To bear witness — to see, and to attest to what one has seen
sh-h-d.field: To see with one's own eyes and to attest to what one has seen: the two gestures are knotted together. The shāhid is not a believer on hearsay — he is the one who was there. Hence shuhūd (contemplation) and shahīd (martyr, whose blood attests).
sh-h-d.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — the root of witness (shahāda) — a remarkably precise unfolding: to attend, to be present, to be witness of, to bear witness, to testify, to observe, to attest, to depose, to confirm, to certify, to acknowledge. The noun shahāda covers, in his lexicon, testimony, attestation, deposition, certificate, citation of proof, the attestation of Islam. And the shahīd he defines as witness, martyr for the faith. For Gloton, then, the Muslim profession of faith (the shahāda) and martyrdom (dying as a shahīd) share their root by no accident: it is the same gesture that attests by word and that attests by life. The mashhad — the place of witness — names, moreover, both the place where one declares and the place where one contemplates: to bear witness is to be present to what one sees. In the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān VI, 19), God Himself is named shahīd — Witness between one human being and another.
sh-h-d.meditation: Does my shahāda rest on what I have heard, or on what something in me has already seen?
forms: to be present, to bear witness, to see
to call to testify; to be taken as a martyr
that which is seen, that to which witness is borne
quran: God bears witness that there is no god but He.
Does it not suffice that your Lord is Witness over all things?

ḥ-m-d.core_fr_original: Praise — recognising the good and voicing it
ḥ-m-d.field: To praise, to render thanks. Distinct from thanksgiving (shukr, for a benefit received), praise (ḥamd) is addressed to what is good in itself. The Quran opens with al-ḥamdu li-Llāh: praise precedes the gift, owed to the very beauty of the One who is.
ḥ-m-d.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root — which gives al-ḥamdu lillāh, the first sentence of the Quran — a core of great simplicity: to praise, to laud, to glorify, to thank, to approve, to render thanks, to find someone worthy of praise, to proclaim someone's qualities. The noun ḥamd is eulogy, praise, thanksgiving, the rendering of thanks. And the intensive form yields Aḥmad and Muḥammad, defined as the one covered with praise, the much-lauded — the name of the Prophet. Thus the very name of the Prophet means the one whom praise never leaves. But the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān III, 188) is a striking warning: not to confuse praise with the desire to be praised — those who love to be praised for what they have not done are precisely those who have not understood what praise is.
ḥ-m-d.meditation: What can I praise at this moment that asks nothing in return?
forms: the Most Worthy of praise (Divine Name)
the most praising / the most praised
the praised station (promised to the Prophet, 17:79)
quran: Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds.
It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praised station.

ṣ-l-w.core_fr: Prayer — running behind to catch up
ṣ-l-w.core_fr_original: Prayer — the ritual bond that holds the vertical
ṣ-l-w.field: The root of the ritual bond. The etymology is debated: ṣilā (the bond) or ṣalan (the bone that flexes in prostration) — the two converge. God Himself “makes ṣalāt” upon the Prophet (33:43): this is not to pray, it is to turn His face with love.
ṣ-l-w.glose_gloton: Gloton offers for this root — that of the ṣalāt, the ritual prayer — a deeply original intuition: the idea of arriving behind someone and catching up with them — to touch, reach or wound someone at the back; to come second in a race, to try to overtake, to effect the bond (of grace) with, to enter into competition, to run after, to gain access to, to pray, to perform prayer, to bless. The noun ṣalāt he defines as prayer, devotion, ritual prayer, an act of uniting grace, the act by which one draws near to God, the effecting of the bond with someone or something, or the reaching of it. To pray, in this intuition, is therefore not to recite — it is to run after the One who is ahead, to effect the bond. Arabic prayer is, etymologically, a catching-up.
ṣ-l-w.meditation: When I pray, am I stretching out a thread — or do I realise it was already stretched toward me?
forms: to pray; (of God:) to bless, to turn His face with love
the ritual prayer; the divine blessing
the prayers (ritual; or: synagogues / places of worship)
quran: It is He who prays upon you (blesses you, turns His face toward you), with His angels, to bring you out of the darknesses into the light.
Indeed, prayer guards against indecency and what is blameworthy.

kh-l-ṣ.core_fr: Sincerity — that which mixes with nothing
kh-l-ṣ.field: To be pure, unmixed, freed. Ikhlāṣ is not “being sincere” in the moral sense — it is to refine one's intention of all that is not Him. Pure metal (khāliṣ) holds no more dross: such must the heart be.
kh-l-ṣ.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition striking in its philosophical precision: the idea of the end of a selective process (a catharsis) — to be wholly white, pure, spotless, limpid, unmixed, freed of all that soiled it. The word ikhlāṣ (sincerity) therefore descends from a process of catharsis: it is not an initial state but the outcome of a long work of purification. Sūra 112 — the sūra of pure uniqueness — bears the name al-Ikhlāṣ: not because it speaks of sincerity, but because it is the refined quintessence of the doctrine of tawḥīd. For Gloton, then, to be sincere is etymologically to have been passed through the fire of selection — to let remain only what mixes with nothing.
kh-l-ṣ.meditation: With what else, at this moment, is my intention to seek Him mixed?
forms: to be pure, to be freed, to come through unharmed
to purify, to save, to release
to elect, to choose for oneself exclusively
exclusive sincerity, the refining of intention
the one who makes his worship sincere
the one whom God has made pure, the chosen
quran: Is it not to God that pure religion belongs?
They were commanded only to worship God, rendering religion to Him exclusively pure.

ʿ-q-l.field: ʿAqala first means to fetter: the rope that ties the camel is ʿiqāl. Intelligence holds the soul back from scattering, knots phenomena into meaning. To understand is to knot — not to look.
ʿ-q-l.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the astonishing genesis of the Arabic intellect: to bind, to hold back, to tie, to join, to ligature, to fetter, to draw tight, to make the link, to link ideas to one another, to understand, to know, to conceive, to show intelligence and discernment. The verb ʿaqala first denotes, concretely, the act of tying the camel with a hobble (ʿiqāl). From this gesture comes the name of intelligence: ʿaql = that which binds. To understand, in Arabic, is to connect. For Gloton, then, intelligence is no abstract faculty — it is the fetter that keeps the nafs from straying. The Quranic formula la-ʿallakum taʿqilūn (“perhaps you will bind together”) is repeated to invite the reader to make the link between the signs.
ʿ-q-l.meditation: What am I letting slip away today, for want of having knotted the meaning?
forms: to fetter, to hold back, to understand
the intelligent one, the one endowed with reason
the intelligible, that which can be grasped
the hobble, the rope that ties the camel
quran: Signs for people who understand (who bind the meaning together).
Had we but listened or understood, we would not be among the inhabitants of the Blaze.

f-h-m.core_fr: The gift of being made one who understands
f-h-m.core_fr_original: To understand — to grasp meaning in depth
f-h-m.field: To understand, to grasp inwardly. Unlike ʿaql (to bind) and fikr (to weigh), fahm is the penetration of meaning: that moment when the veil falls, when one sees what one was hearing without hearing. Solomon is made fahīm (21:79).
f-h-m.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a remarkably direct core: to understand, to hear, to grasp by the intelligence, to conceive, to seize the meaning, to interpret. The active verbal form (fahhama) means to make someone understand, grasp or know, to render intelligible. The Quran, in the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān XXI, 79), uses it of Solomon: We made Solomon understand the matter. To understand, in the Arabic root, is never a solitary conquest — it is always a reception of what has been given to be understood.
f-h-m.meditation: What, read a hundred times, still asks to be given to me to understand?
forms: to seek to understand, to study
the one who understands in depth
quran: We made Solomon understand [the judgement]. To each We gave wisdom and knowledge.

f-k-r.core_fr_original: Thought — to weigh, to turn over, to meditate
f-k-r.field: Fakara, tafakkara: to turn a thing over in one's mind, to weigh it, to consider it from several angles. Fikr is not logical thought — it is the act of meditating, of circling a sign as one circles a mystery.
f-k-r.glose_gloton: Gloton sets out the degrees of thought with great finesse: to think, to reflect, to meditate, to reason, to cogitate, to deliberate, to speculate, to consider. The intensive form (tafakkara) adds: to meditate, to reflect thoroughly, to consider a question in all its aspects, to examine a theme of reflection at length, to perform an act of introspection. And the Quran cited by Gloton (Qurʾān XXX, 21) closes the root upon meditation as the right attitude: in that there are indeed Signs for those who meditate. To think, in Quranic Arabic, is to inhabit a thought, not to pass through it.
f-k-r.meditation: Which of today's signs deserves that I circle it several times?
forms: to give oneself to meditation, to turn over in one's mind
quran: And they meditate upon the creation of the heavens and the earth.
Such are the parables We set forth for humankind, that they may meditate.

ʿ-r-f.core_fr: To know — to gain height, to catch the fragrance
ʿ-r-f.core_fr_original: To know by familiarity, by taste
ʿ-r-f.field: To know by recognition, by familiarity — not by learned knowledge. One knows a face because one has already seen it. The maʿrifa of God is of this order: not to know things about Him, but to re-cognise Him when He shows Himself.
ʿ-r-f.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root, which yields maʿrifa (Sufi gnosis), a magnificent intuition: the idea of knowing by distinguishing, by gaining height, of spreading, of diffusing (a fragrance), of catching scents — to know, to learn, to recognise and distinguish, to discern, to acknowledge. The noun ʿurf he defines as that which one knows or recognises, established usage, customs and practices, customary law, tradition, a high and recognised place, height, acknowledgement, knowledge. And Gloton points to a nearly forgotten dimension: the same root gives aʿrāf (the Heights), the name of a Quranic sūra — heights, edges, fringes, frontiers, crests, elevated places, eminences, hills, limits, boundary markers. To know, in Arabic, is to climb high enough to distinguish — and to catch, like a fragrance, what diffuses from the invisible.
ʿ-r-f.meditation: What I think I am discovering today — is it not rather something I recognise?
forms: the knower (Sufi term for the gnostic)
the known (and hence: what is well known, the fitting, the right)
quran: And We have made you into peoples and tribes, that you may know one another.
And He will admit them into the Garden He has made known to them.

ḥ-k-m.core_fr: Wisdom — the bit that keeps to the way
ḥ-k-m.core_fr_original: Wisdom — to judge rightly, to set each thing in its place
ḥ-k-m.field: The first sense: to put on a curb. The horse's bit is ḥakama. Wisdom is the mastery that sets each thing in its just place. To judge, to govern, to be wise: three modes of a single art of measure.
ḥ-k-m.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root a vertiginous breadth: the idea of establishing an enduring rule of wisdom, of observing a norm, of maintaining a rule, of keeping to the way, of fulfilling the universal Law — to arbitrate, to judge, to decide, to declare, to determine, to settle a dispute, to condemn to, to consolidate, to make firm — to muzzle a horse and set the martingale beneath its chin, to hold the course, to hold back, to restrain and prevent from acting. This last image — the horse's bit — is central: ḥikma (wisdom) shares its root with that which holds back, which keeps to the way. The wise one, in this intuition, is not the one who knows — it is the one who holds his direction without straying into the passions of the moment. Al-Ḥakīm, the Wise, is one of the Names of God: the One who never deviates.
ḥ-k-m.meditation: What thing, at this moment, is not in its just place — and what would need to be moved?
forms: to judge, to govern, to decide
to make solid, to do well, to perfect
to bring a dispute before a judge
to grow consolidated, to become firm
the firm, the clear (a verse of unequivocal meaning)
quran: He gives wisdom to whom He wills, and whoever is given wisdom has received a great good.
We gave Luqmān wisdom: Be grateful to God.

ʿ-d-l.field: To be equitable, balanced, upright. The root evokes the scales with their pans level (iʿtidāl). The sky “was raised according to a balance” (55:7): Quranic justice is cosmological before it is juridical.
ʿ-d-l.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of juridical and metaphysical breadth: to be just, to pronounce with justice, to decide in full justice, to make equal, to be equivalent, to treat with equity, to balance, to adjust. The noun ʿadl covers justice, equity, balance, adjustment, that which weighs equally. For Gloton, Arabic justice is above all a balance of the two pans — not an abstract moral verdict but a set of scales. And — remarkably — the root also yields, in its antonym, the verb to swerve, to deviate: ḍ-l-l is precisely the deviation from the equilibrium of ʿ-d-l.
ʿ-d-l.meditation: Where am I tilting today — and what would it take to return to the centre?
forms: to equal, to set in balance
to stand upright, to reach equilibrium
quran: Indeed, God commands justice and excellence.
And the sky, He raised it, and He set down the balance: that you may not transgress in the balance.

z-k-y.field: Zakā knots two senses together: to grow, to prosper and to purify oneself. The beauty lies in the unity: to purify is to make grow. The zakāt draws from one's wealth not to diminish it, but to make it prosper.
z-k-y.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an intuition of great beauty: to be pure, spotless, to grow, to increase, to augment, to be fitting, to live in well-being, to take delight in a place. Purity and growth share the same root: what is pure grows, and what grows is purified by its very growth. The noun zakāt (the obligatory alms) is defined as purity, legal alms, a surplus of goods distributed which purifies them, a purifying tax on certain goods. To give to the poor, in Islam, is no incidental charity: it is the operation that renders pure what one keeps. Wealth that does not circulate corrupts; wealth that gives part of itself away increases the rest.
z-k-y.meditation: What do I believe I am losing by giving a portion away — when it is precisely that which would make it grow?
forms: to grow, to prosper, to be pure
the obligatory alms (lit.: that which makes grow)
the pure one, the prosperous one
quran: He succeeds who purifies it (the soul), he fails who buries it.
He purifies them and teaches them the Book and wisdom.

t-q-y.core_fr: The vigilant attentiveness of the heart
t-q-y.core_fr_original: To preserve oneself — the fear that protects
t-q-y.field: The primary root is w-q-y: to preserve. Ittaqā: to take shelter, to be on guard. Taqwā, poorly translated as “fear of God,” is more exactly the vigilant awareness that preserves. A loving care, not a servile fear.
t-q-y.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to taqwā its concrete ground before its religious sense: to be on guard, to seek to avoid, to guard oneself against, to fear, to protect oneself, to be attentive, to be vigilant, to be wary, to apprehend. The noun taqwā he defines as guarding, precaution, wariness, prudence, circumspection, apprehension. For Gloton, then, the Quranic taqwā — usually rendered as “piety” or “fear of God” — is rather a vigilant attentiveness, an awakened guard of the heart. It is not servile fear; it is the loving vigilance of one who wants to do nothing that would displease the Beloved.
t-q-y.meditation: What do I truly need to preserve myself from today — not out of fear, but out of care for the essential?
forms: to guard oneself, to be on guard, to fear
the protecting awareness, the “fear” of God
the one who guards himself, the vigilant and pious one
quran: Indeed, the noblest of you before God is the most muttaqī (the most vigilant).
Take provision: the best provision is taqwā.

kh-sh-y.core_fr: Reverential awe — to tremble out of nearness
kh-sh-y.field: A fear that is not dread, but the trembling of one who knows himself before something greater than himself. Unlike khawf (fear of a possible harm) and taqwā (vigilance), khashya is purely reverential — it is born of knowledge.
kh-sh-y.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a gradation of fear: to fear, to be wary, to be afraid, to be alarmed, to take fright, to apprehend, to dread, to be anxious. But — and this is essential — Quranic khashya is distinguished from simple fear (khawf) in this: it is accompanied by knowledge. The verse says so explicitly: among His servants, only the learned (al-ʿulamāʾ) hold God in awe (yakhshā) (35:28). For Gloton, then, khashya is the fear of the one who knows — the fear born of an acute awareness of the divine rank, not of an external threat. It is to tremble out of nearness, not out of distance.
kh-sh-y.meditation: Is there in me a knowledge that no longer trembles — and what, then, is it worth?
forms: to fear with reverence, to hold in awe
the one who holds in reverential awe
humble (a close cognate, of the same semantic field)
quran: Among His servants, only the learned hold God in awe.
Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled, splitting apart out of awe of God.

ṣ-d-q.core_fr: The spontaneous offering — truth and almsgiving
ṣ-d-q.core_fr_original: Truthfulness — the accord of word, deed and being
ṣ-d-q.field: To speak truly. But the root demands more: the conformity of word, act and being. The ṣiddīq (the supreme rank after prophet) is the one whose life attests what his mouth says. Ṣadaqa = alms: the gift, too, attests.
ṣ-d-q.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root — which speaks truthfulness and almsgiving — onto a deep intuition of their unity: the idea of the offering of oneself or of one's goods in full spontaneity, without reserve — to be true, sincere, truthful, authentic, loyal, upright, frank, genuine, to realise, to fulfil, to give credence to someone, to acknowledge without reserve, to be right, to speak the truth. For Gloton, then, sincerity (ṣidq) and almsgiving (ṣadaqa) are not two different things: they are the two faces of a single gesture — the spontaneous and unreserved offering. To speak the truth is to give oneself by what one says. To give to the poor is to speak truly by what one has. The liar holds back; the miser holds back; both, in reality, lie at the same place in the heart.
ṣ-d-q.meditation: What I have just said — do my body and my acts make it true?
forms: to speak truly, to be truthful
to give credence to, to confirm
quran: And mention in the Book Abraham — he was utterly truthful, a prophet.
Among the believers are men who have made true (by their deeds) what they promised to God.

ʾ-d-b.core_fr: Spiritual courtesy — the fitting of the gesture to the sacred
ʾ-d-b.field: Adab has no equivalent: neither politeness, nor morality, nor culture. It is the inner and outer fitting to the situation, the person, the moment. Adab of the disciple, adab of prayer, adab toward God: each register has its own.
ʾ-d-b.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an astonishing unfolding that joins discipline and garden: to tame, to train through exercises; a well-arranged garden, well cultivated and prosperous, a rich and ordered orchard, a rich flowerbed, prosperous vegetation. The word adab therefore covers discipline, exercise, civility, good manners, courtesy, refinement — but also banquet, feast. The central intuition is limpid: spiritual courtesy is the ordering of conduct, as a garden is ordered. For Gloton, then, Sufi adab is the art of cultivating one's behaviour like an orchard — pruning what overreaches, watering what grows straight, and offering the resulting beauty as a banquet to the divine guest.
ʾ-d-b.meditation: What gesture, this morning, asks to be fitted — not by rule, but by regard?
forms: to educate, to form in adab
to form oneself, to acquire adab
the cultivated person, the person of adab
quran: My Lord has educated me and perfected my education. (a prophetic saying)

kh-l-q.core_fr: To create — and: character, what is created within the human being
kh-l-q.field: The splendour of the root: a single word, khalq, names both creation and character. My character is my share of creation — what God has shaped in me and what I must, in turn, shape. “A character immense” (68:4) is the praise of the Prophet.
kh-l-q.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto an artisan's gesture: to give measure, proportion or norm to; to take the measure (without cutting); to cut to shape; to fashion; to create by giving form. For Gloton, Arabic creation is no abstract act ex nihilo — it is to give measure, to fashion with proportion. And — a capital detail — the same root yields khulq: character, manners, natural disposition. A person's character (akhlāq) is, etymologically, what has been created within them, their inner form. For the Sufi, then, to work on one's character and to take part in the divine creation are a single discipline.
kh-l-q.meditation: My character is a share of creation — what can I carry forward in it of what God began there?
forms: the intense Creator, the One who ceaselessly fashions
quran: And indeed, you are of an immense character.
Blessed be God, the best of creators.

g-f-r.core_fr: Forgiveness — to cover with a protecting veil
g-f-r.field: Ghafara = to cover, to veil. The warrior's helmet is mighfar. Forgiveness is not erasure: it is covering over. God lays a veil over the fault, which does not disappear but ceases to act.
g-f-r.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of perfect aptness: to cover, to cover over (faults, sins), to let them appear no more, to conceal, to hide away. Arabic forgiveness (maghfira) is therefore not an erasure — it is a covering. The word mighfar denotes, moreover, the helmet that protects the head in battle: that which truly covers. When the servant asks ighfir lī, he is not asking God to act as though the fault had not occurred — he is asking Him to cover the fault, to veil it, to render it invisible. Al-Ghafūr (the One who covers over) is the Divine Name that bears the fullness of this.
g-f-r.meditation: What, within me, asks not to be erased, but simply covered?
forms: to forgive, to cover with a veil
to ask forgiveness, to ask for the covering
the One who never ceases to forgive
the helmet (that which covers the head)
quran: Indeed, God forgives all sins. He is the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful.
Ask forgiveness of your Lord — He has ever been the One who never ceases to forgive.

h-d-y.core_fr: The walk in a straight line
h-d-y.core_fr_original: Guidance — the gift of the way
h-d-y.field: To guide, to lead. The root carries the way (hudā) and the present one offers (hadiyya): to guide is to give the most precious of gifts. No one guides himself — it is God who guides whom He wills (28:56).
h-d-y.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root its central axis: to be well directed, to be in — or to follow — the right direction, to be orthodox. Hudā (right direction) is defined as right direction, right action, the walk in a straight line, uprightness, an upright way of acting. In the verse Gloton cites (Qurʾān II, 256), it stands explicitly against deviation (al-ghayy): right direction is surely distinguished from deviation. To be well directed, in the Quranic perspective, is not to follow a morality — it is to let oneself be oriented by that which orients, as the compass needle by magnetic north. Rectitude is not an effort but a consent.
h-d-y.meditation: If guidance is a gift, have I held out my hands to receive it today?
forms: to let oneself be guided, to find the way
quran: Guide us upon the straight way.
You do not guide those you love — it is God who guides whom He wills.

ḍ-l-l.core_fr: Straying — to lose one's way and not know it
ḍ-l-l.field: To go astray, to lose one's way. The root evokes the one who still walks but no longer knows where. Not a halt, but a disoriented walking. The exact opposite of hudā. The Quran: “He found you wandering, and He guided you” (93:7).
ḍ-l-l.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root — which speaks of spiritual straying — onto a very concrete ground: to turn off the road and go astray, to lose one's way, to be dispossessed, to suffer a loss, to be damaged, to come off worse, to be mistaken in a matter of trade. The noun ḍalāl covers miscalculation, loss, prejudice, the damage suffered, disappointment. Quranic straying, in Gloton's reading, is therefore no abstract sin: it is a commercial loss, a disappointment, the bargain in which one thought oneself the winner and lost everything. And — a precious fact — the root carries, in itself, no moral connotation. Straying is first to take the wrong road — which can happen to anyone, and is grave only if one persists in not acknowledging it.
ḍ-l-l.meditation: In what part of myself am I still walking — but without knowing toward what?
forms: to go astray, to lose the trail
to lead astray, to make lose the way
the one who has gone astray
quran: The way of those You have blessed — not of those who incur anger, nor of those who have gone astray.
He found you wandering, and He guided you.

j-h-d.core_fr: The effort that does not spare itself
j-h-d.core_fr_original: Effort — to deploy one's whole capacity
j-h-d.field: Jahada: to toil, to deploy one's juhd (maximal capacity). The total engagement of oneself in a direction. Jihād, poorly translated as “holy war,” is above all sustained effort — inward first (al-jihād al-akbar).
j-h-d.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root its first concreteness before its spiritual sense: to strive, to apply oneself, to insist, to work with zeal and diligence, to make an effort toward, to exhaust, to wear out. The noun jihād covers effort, application, vigour, combat, struggle. For Gloton, the root therefore speaks first of the fatigue of effort — work to the point of exhaustion, total engagement in a task. It is upon this concrete ground that the struggle against oneself then unfolds (the jihād akbar, the great inner combat) and, only by a second derivation, outward combat. The root, in itself, does not set spiritual against material: it speaks of unreserved investment.
j-h-d.meditation: Where, within me, have I not yet deployed my whole capacity?
forms: to toil, to make an effort
to deploy a sustained effort, to struggle
to make a personal effort (notably intellectual)
sustained effort on the way of God
the one who spends himself, the inner combatant
the one who exercises his own effort
quran: And those who strive for Us, We shall guide upon Our ways.
And strive in God with the true striving due to Him.

n-ṣ-r.core_fr_original: Succour — to come to the aid of
n-ṣ-r.field: To succour, to come to the aid of. Quranic naṣr is not victory alone — it is any succour that arrives in time. The Anṣār are the Medinan companions; naṣrāniyy (Christian) comes from the same root: those who succoured Jesus.
n-ṣ-r.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an astonishing unfolding: to help, to succour, to assist, to defend someone, to deliver, to grant victory, to support, to second, to be an auxiliary, to lend one's backing, to water abundantly. This last nuance — to water abundantly — is central: naṣr (succour) shares its root with the image of water descending upon the parched earth. To help, in Quranic Arabic, is to water — not to give from above, but to make a water descend that penetrates. And a remarkable fact Gloton underlines: the same root yields naṣārā, the Christians or Nazarenes. For the Quranic tongue, Christianity is, etymologically, the community of those who succour.
n-ṣ-r.meditation: Where do I need to be succoured today — and where am I called to succour?
forms: to be victorious, to be granted succour
quran: If you succour God, He will succour you and make firm your steps.
When the succour of God comes, and the opening.

gh-y-b.core_fr: The Unattested — faith in the hidden
gh-y-b.core_fr_original: Mystery — that which is absent to sight
gh-y-b.field: To be absent, hidden. The ghayb is neither nothingness nor falsehood — it is what is, but does not appear. The pairing al-ghayb wa-sh-shahāda covers the whole of the real: what I see, and what is without appearing to me.
gh-y-b.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root with a breadth that exceeds ordinary absence: to be absent, hidden, far from, invisible, mysterious, extinguished, to set, to disappear, to vanish, to absent oneself, to conceal oneself, to withdraw. The noun ghayb covers absence, remoteness, disappearance, secret, mystery, all that is hidden, unmanifested, unattested — the “Unattested.” Gloton even coins the French word l'Inattesté (the Unattested) to render this Quranic dimension of what is, by its essence, withdrawn from all human attestation. In the programmatic verse he cites (Qurʾān II, 3): those who carry faith in the Unattested. To believe, in Arabic, is first to have faith in what can be neither seen nor proven.
gh-y-b.meditation: What part of the real is, at this moment, here without appearing to me — and which I might simply greet?
forms: to slander (to speak of someone in their absence)
intensive: the One who knows the invisible perfectly
quran: Those who believe in the mystery (in the ghayb).
The Knower of the invisible and the visible.

ḥ-y-y.field: To be alive. Strangely, the same root yields ḥayāʾ (modesty) — as though to be alive and to have modesty were linked: what is alive holds itself in reserve. Al-Ḥayy is the One who stands, and who upholds all that lives.
ḥ-y-y.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition of astonishing concision: to live, to blush, to feel shame. Three senses in three words — and their bond is philosophically profound. To live, in Arabic, is to have the capacity to blush, that is, to be aware of the gaze of the other. The dead one feels no shame because he no longer feels the gaze. Al-Ḥayy (the Living) is one of the Divine Names; and al-ḥayāʾ (modesty, reverential shame) is, in the hadith, a branch of faith. For Gloton, then, to be alive and to be capable of blushing before God are, etymologically, the same state.
ḥ-y-y.meditation: At this moment, what within me truly stands — and what is sagging?
quran: God — there is no god but He — the Living, the Self-Subsisting.
Indeed, that One is the One who gives life to the dead — He has power over all things.

m-w-t.field: To die. The opposite and complementary pole of life. The Quran avoids tragic dualism: “He who created death and life” (67:2) — death is created, hence willed, hence good. The path speaks of a death before death.
m-w-t.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking descending chain: to come to an end, to be extinguished, to perish, to cease living, to succumb, to give up the soul, to pass away, to grow calm (of the wind). And — a revealing detail — he notes that the same root applies to the wind that grows calm. To die, in Arabic, is to grow calm like a wind: the breath (nafas) that passed through one ceases to pass. The Name al-Mumīt (the One who gives death) descends from this root: God does not kill violently in Quranic language — He makes cease. Death is therefore, for Gloton, the final stilling of the breath.
m-w-t.meditation: To what within me am I called to die so that something else may live?
forms: to give death, to cause to die
quran: He who created death and life to test you — which of you would be best in deed.
Every soul will taste death.

b-ṣ-r.core_fr: Vision — to see with the inner eye
b-ṣ-r.field: To see, to perceive. The root distinguishes two modes: baṣar (physical sight) and baṣīra (inner vision). The Quran insists on the possible blindness of the baṣīra while the eyes still function: “it is the hearts that are blind” (22:46).
b-ṣ-r.glose_gloton: Gloton opens the root onto a twofold movement of vision: to see clearly, to look, to grasp intuitively, to be clear-sighted, to understand. The noun baṣar covers sight, gaze, clear-sightedness, penetration, perspicacity, intelligence. And baṣīra he specifies as inner sight, the faculty of penetration, intuition, clear proof, attention, care. For Gloton, then, Arabic vision unfolds on two levels: the physical eye (baṣar) and the inner eye (baṣīra). The saint, the sage, the prophet possess baṣīra at the highest degree — seeing not only things, but the meaning of things. And the verb tabaṣṣara in the fifth form: to observe, to consider, to seek to see clearly — the active effort of the gaze. Al-Baṣīr (the All-Seeing) is a Divine Name: God sees not only the appearance but the inward.
b-ṣ-r.meditation: My eyes see — but what does my heart truly look upon at this moment?
forms: to see, to catch sight of
quran: It is not the eyes that are blind, but the hearts within the breasts.
Say: This is my way — I call to God in full clear-sightedness, I and those who follow me.

s-m-ʿ.core_fr: Hearing — to hear, to listen, to obey
s-m-ʿ.field: Three knotted acts: to hear, to listen, to obey. In the Semitic root, to truly listen is to respond. Samiʿnā wa aṭaʿnā (“we have heard and obeyed,” 2:285) condenses faith into two words. Samāʿ = Sufi spiritual audition.
s-m-ʿ.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a chain of acts running from simple hearing to obedience: to listen, to hear, to give ear, to obey, to follow an opinion, to understand. A striking thing: in Arabic, to hear and to obey share their root — the one who truly listens, follows. The famous Quranic formula samiʿnā wa-aṭaʿnā (“we have heard and we obey,” 2:285) condenses the root: to hear is to begin to obey. As-Samīʿ — the Hearing — is one of the Divine Names: God does not listen passively; His presence to what He hears is already an act.
s-m-ʿ.meditation: If to hear is to respond — what have I heard today that I have not yet answered?
quran: And they said: We have heard and we have obeyed — Your forgiveness, our Lord.
I am with you both — I hear and I see.

w-j-h.core_fr_original: The face — orientation, that which turns toward
w-j-h.field: Wajh denotes the face, the direction one takes, the aspect under which a thing presents itself. The “face of God” is what in Him turns toward us — not an anthropomorphism, but divine orientation itself. “All perishes save His face” (28:88).
w-j-h.glose_gloton: Gloton restores for wajh its whole web of meaning: face, countenance, the front side, the top, the right side, façade, the part of a thing that faces outward, appearance, aspect, direction, orientation, manner, way, mode, means, the chief place, honour, dignity, a notable, a leader, a beginning, intention, aim, the essence of a thing, bearing. The face, in Arabic, is therefore not one bodily feature among others: it is the part that faces, the essence, the intention, the aim. The Face of God (wajh Allāh) celebrated by the Quran is thus at once His Presence and His orientation toward us — what He turns toward us, and what we turn toward Him in return. The face is the organ par excellence of turning toward.
w-j-h.meditation: Toward what is my face turned at this moment — and where would I want it to turn back?
forms: to turn toward, to orient oneself
the face, the direction, the aspect
dignity, rank (lit.: the quality of the face)
quran: Wherever you turn, there is the face of God.
All things perish, save His face.

k-b-r.core_fr: To magnify — to make greater by the word
k-b-r.field: Kabura: to be great. A twofold root: applied to God, it speaks His transcendence (Allāhu akbar); applied to the human being, it speaks pride (takabbur) — the illegitimate claim to greatness. Iblīs falls through istikbār.
k-b-r.glose_gloton: Gloton restores to this root — which yields al-Kabīr (the Most Great, a Divine Name) and the formula Allāhu akbar — a striking unfolding: the idea of great quantity, of importance, of greatness — to be older, to be great, to be of large build, to be strong, to increase, to grow, to be grave, important, magnificent, superb, considerable, to appear enormous. The noun covers greatness, importance, magnificence, glory, eminence, majesty, pomp, splendour, prestige, distinction, nobility — and enormity, pride, conceit. A remarkable trait: the same root bears the greatness of God and the pride of the human being. And the verb takbīr (proclaiming Allāhu akbar) Gloton defines as the proclamation of someone's greatness, magnification. To say Allāhu akbar is therefore, etymologically, to magnify — in the primary sense of the verb: to make greater by the word what was already great in itself. The language itself keeps the fruitful ambiguity: only the one who has renounced magnifying himself has the right to magnify God.
k-b-r.meditation: When I say Allāhu akbar, what within me truly consents not to be great?
forms: to grow proud, to make oneself great
to swell with pride, to claim greatness
the proclamation of divine greatness (Allāhu akbar)
the One who possesses greatness as His own (Divine Name); or: the proud one (of the human being)
quran: And proclaim His greatness with a true proclamation.
He refused, swelled with pride, and was among the ungrateful.

k-t-b.core_fr: The written word that revives the imaginal
k-t-b.core_fr_original: Writing — that which is traced, ordered, prescribed
k-t-b.field: To write, to trace, to prescribe. Three layers: the act (kitāba), the book (kitāb), the prescription (kutiba ʿalaykum). To write is not only to record — it is to inscribe in the real, to fix a destiny. All is already written.
k-t-b.glose_gloton: Gloton uncovers here a rare intuition: the root of writing also carries mythic memory. To write, to cut, to throw down (to the ground), to compose a book, to trace lines or characters, to keep a register, to relate ancient narratives, to call to mind myths, primordial scenes. And he specifies, for the form asāṭīr (which ordinary translation renders as “fables”): myths, traditional primitive or primordial narratives that remain alive in human memory or the human imaginal, tales, ancient stories, fables. For Gloton, then, to write is not only to record — it is to revive the imaginal, to keep alive the memory of what was at the beginning. The Book, in Islam, is no archive: it is a present that renews itself with each reading.
k-t-b.meditation: What line, within me, is being written today — by my choices?
forms: to have something written, to transcribe
quran: This is the Book — no doubt in it — a guidance for the vigilant.
Fasting has been prescribed (written) for you.

m-l-k.core_fr: Sovereignty — to hold and to knead
m-l-k.field: To possess, to reign. Three key words: malik (the reigning king), mālik (the master who possesses), mulk (sovereignty). The Quran uses both of God: al-Malik and Mālik al-Mulk. The fātiḥa says Mālik yawm ad-dīn.
m-l-k.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root — that of al-Malik (the King, a Divine Name) and of mulk (sovereignty) — a revealing breadth: to contain, to hold, to possess, to detain, to make use of something, to master, to keep in one's possession, to have a thing for one's use, to control, to make oneself master of, to exercise authority, to reign, to dominate, to conquer, to govern, to marry (a woman), to knead (dough). The last nuance — to knead dough — is striking: to reign, within this root, is to hold a matter in hand to the point of working it. Mulk covers authority, reign, empire, sovereignty, greatness, magnificence, mastery, power, possession, property, the right of property, what one owns as one's own. And Gloton signals a remarkable etymological fact: the word malak (angel) may come from the non-Quranic root L-ʾ-K, to send, to commission, with the prefix ma- marking nouns of instrument and function. The angels are therefore not kings by lexical confusion: they are envoys whose root is distinct, and the near-homophony with mulk is one of the miracles proper to the Arabic tongue.
m-l-k.meditation: Over what do I believe I reign in my life — that does not, in truth, belong to me?
forms: the kingdom, the royal dimension of the real
quran: Master of the Day of Judgement.
Blessed be the One in whose hand is sovereignty — He has power over all things.

r-b-b.core_fr_original: The Lord who educates and makes grow
r-b-b.field: Rabb is not an abstract “lord.” The root evokes the one who educates, who makes grow — hence rabba (to raise a child), tarbiya (education). God is Rabb: the One who makes each being grow toward its completion.
r-b-b.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this founding root a rare breadth: the idea of mastering and educating — to be master, chief, lord; to have control of, to exercise authority, to gather, to assemble, to arrange, to accommodate, to raise, to educate, to possess, to be an owner, the idea of permanence, to perfume something. The rabb he defines as master, lord, educator, the one who teaches with authority and competence, an instructor, a possessor, an owner. And Gloton, in his translation of the verse al-ḥamdu lillāhi rabbi l-ʿālamīn (Qurʾān I, 2), deliberately chooses: Praise be to Allāh, the Educator (or = the Master) of the beings of the Universe. The Lord is not, within this root, an overlord — He is an educator who raises His creature in the primary sense: He makes it grow.
r-b-b.meditation: In what am I being educated, at this very moment, by the One who makes me grow without my seeing it?
forms: to raise, to educate, to make grow
the spiritual master (who educates through God)
quran: Praise be to God, Lord (educator) of the worlds.
And say: My Lord, have mercy upon them, as they raised me when I was small.

w-l-y.core_fr: Extreme nearness — the saint, the intimate friend
w-l-y.field: To be near, allied, protecting. Walī = intimate friend, ally, and among the Sufis the saint (the one near to God). Walāya is the quality of the walī. God is al-Walī: the One nearest, the protector of the believers.
w-l-y.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root, which yields the word walī (intimate friend, saint), onto an intuition of pure nearness: to be very near, contiguous, to be very close, to follow very closely, to be within hand's reach, to take charge of something, to be appointed over, to be the intimate friend of someone, to govern, to administer, to follow on, to defend someone. The walī he defines as the very near, the immediate, the appointed one, friend, protector, intimate, neighbour, contiguous, aid, auxiliary, ally, associate, partisan, the very near master, companion, benefactor, guardian — one of the Names of God. For Gloton, the saint (walī Allāh) is therefore not an extraordinary human being: he is the one to whom God has made Himself very near, by a movement that descends, not by a movement that climbs.
w-l-y.meditation: Do I let the One who wants to be near make Himself near to me today?
forms: to be near, to govern, to take care
to ally oneself, to make oneself near
to take charge of; to turn away (according to context)
the Near, the Ally, the Saint
the saints, those near to God
the Master, the Protector, the supreme Friend
quran: God is the Walī (Near-protector) of those who believe — He brings them out of the darknesses into the light.
The awliyāʾ (the near ones) of God — no fear is upon them, nor grief.

b-y-n.core_fr_original: Clear exposition — that which separates and makes visible
b-y-n.field: To appear clearly. A twofold tension: bayna (between, the space that separates) and bayān (the exposition that makes clear). For a thing to appear, a gap must open. The Quran al-mubīn separates the true from the false.
b-y-n.glose_gloton: Gloton sets out for this root a range of great precision: to see clearly, to look, to grasp intuitively, to be clear-sighted, to understand. The noun baṣīra (clear-sightedness) covers, in his lexicon, inner sight, the faculty of penetration, intuition, clear proof, attention, care. Where French must choose between seeing and understanding, Arabic holds them together: to see truly is already to understand — and the one who understands has first seen. Arabic clear-sightedness is not an opinion but an organ.
b-y-n.meditation: What asks, within me, to become clear today — even at the price of a separation?
forms: to make clear, to set forth
to become clear, to be made manifest
to grow clear, to become evident
quran: He created the human being — He taught him clear exposition.
Rectitude has become clearly distinct from straying.

q-d-r.core_fr: Measure — the decree, the just portion
q-d-r.field: To measure, to fix, to decree. Two linked layers: qudra (power, capacity) and qadar (measure, decree). All that is, is measured: “We have created every thing according to a measure” (54:49). “Destiny” is not fatality — it is a just portion.
q-d-r.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a striking unfolding that joins measure and authority: to determine, to prescribe, to decree, to settle, to decide, to judge, to ordain, to command, to accomplish, to fix the measure, to have power, to be capable, to know how to measure. The noun qadr covers measure, just proportion, decree, order, power, capacity, value, dignity. For Gloton, qadr is never arbitrary: it is the just measure of each thing. The divine decree (qadar) is no blind fatality — it is the exact adjustment God gives to each being. The Night of Destiny (Laylat al-Qadr) takes its name from the same root: the night on which the measure of the year is decided.
q-d-r.meditation: And what if what comes to me today were neither chance nor punishment — but the just measure of this moment?
forms: to measure, to fix, to be able
to be powerful, to have capacity
quran: We have created every thing according to a measure.
We sent it down on the Night of the Decree.

kh-y-r.core_fr: The good — and the act of choosing
kh-y-r.field: Khāra: to be good; ikhtāra: to choose. A single root for the good and the choice — as though to choose were to go toward the good. The istikhāra is the act by which the believer asks God for the good choice.
kh-y-r.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root a definition that joins quality and choice: to obtain something good; to be favourable, propitious; to prefer, to choose, to give preference to. The noun khayr covers the good, the fine, wealth, excellence, that which one would choose. For Gloton, to choose and to obtain the good share their root: to choose truly is to choose what is good. Ikhtiyār (free choice) is therefore, etymologically, the choice of the good. And the recurrent Quranic formula khayr lakum (“better for you”) recalls that God chooses for His servant what is good — even when the servant would prefer otherwise.
kh-y-r.meditation: What I prefer — is it truly the good, or only what pleases me?
forms: to ask God for the best choice
the good, that which has worth
quran: And your Lord creates what He wills and chooses. The choice does not belong to them.
It may be that you dislike a thing that is a good for you.

kh-l-d.field: To last, to abide without end. Khulūd is eternal duration — that promised to the Garden (jannāt al-khuld, 25:15), dreadful for whoever clings to the Fire. What is eternal is so because God wills it thus: suspended upon His permanence.
