kh-l-d.glose_gloton: Gloton draws this root tight around its central intuition: the idea of eternity, of having no end in time, of permanence. The word khulūd he precisely glosses: eternity, timelessness, outside time, without temporal limit, permanence. The verse he cites (Qurʾān XXVIII, 71–72) is striking: God asks the creature what it would do if the night were permanent, or the day permanent. Permanence is thus thought of not as a state desirable in itself, but as a terrible possibility — one that would render unbearable what, by its ending, becomes precious. True eternity, Gloton suggests, is not the absence of an end: it is the quality of being of the One who includes time without being touched by it.
kh-l-d.meditation: What, within me, would want to last forever — and what consents to pass away?
forms: to make eternal; to cling (to the earth)
quran: Say: Is that better, or the Garden of eternity promised to the vigilant?
Abiding therein forever, as long as the heavens and the earth endure.

j-n-n.core_fr: The hidden garden — to cover, to shelter, to veil
j-n-n.field: Janna = to cover, to veil, to shelter. In a cascade: jannā (garden, foliage that covers), janīn (the hidden embryo), jinn (the invisible ones), junūn (madness, the veiled mind). All speak of what is held hidden.
j-n-n.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the matrixial intuition: the idea of covering over, of being possessed — to cover, to envelop, to darken, to hide, to be rendered invisible. All the striking derivatives of this root descend from the same gesture: janna (the garden, covered with vegetation); jinn (the beings hidden from the human gaze); junūn (madness, in which the mind is covered over); janīn (the embryo, covered within the womb). For Gloton, then, the paradisal garden and the hidden being share their structure: both are covered over. When the Quran promises the janna to the servants, it is not only a garden of delight — it is a place where one is enveloped, sheltered, veiled from the ordinary gaze.
j-n-n.meditation: The Garden is covered, not absent — where, here below, do I sense its hidden depth?
forms: to cover, to veil, to shelter
quran: And hasten toward the forgiveness of your Lord and a garden wide as the heavens and the earth.
And for the one who has feared the standing before his Lord, two gardens.

n-z-l.core_fr: Descent — a coming from on high
n-z-l.field: To descend. Revelation is tanzīl or nuzūl: the Word descends from the Most High. Manzil = the place where one alights, a stage of the journey. Rain too descends. Every true gift comes from on high.
n-z-l.glose_gloton: Gloton opens this root onto a concrete movement of descent: to roll and fall down, to lower, to lower oneself, to decline, to diminish, to decrease, to fall, to descend, to alight, to cast down, to lessen, to come down. And — strikingly — the same root yields to make descend, to bring down from on high. The descent of the Quran (tanzīl al-Qurʾān) is spoken by this root: not a simple sending, but a graduated fall from heaven toward earth. And manzil (a stage, a provisional dwelling) comes from it too: it is the place where one alights. The Sufi traveller walks from one manzil to the next.
n-z-l.meditation: What would want to descend upon me at this moment, if I fell silent?
forms: the place where one alights, the stage, the dwelling
that which is made to descend (the revelation)
quran: It is by the True that We sent it down, and by the True that it descended.
The angels and the Breath descend therein.

ʿ-r-j.core_fr: To climb by degrees — the arduous ascent
ʿ-r-j.field: To climb, to ascend. Miʿrāj = ladder, degree, ascensional way — and par excellence the Prophet's nocturnal ascension. The pairing nuzūl / ʿurūj structures the cosmology: revelation descends, the soul climbs.
ʿ-r-j.glose_gloton: Gloton gives this root — that of the miʿrāj, the Prophet's celestial ascension — a striking bodily core: to climb by degrees, to climb a ladder, to move forward gradually, to ascend a slope with effort, to haul oneself up, to clamber, to scale, to walk with a halting, jerky gait. The noun covers sustained efforts to ascend a slope, terrible torment. And the definition widens: that which slopes upward, the highest part of a steep mountain, an insurmountable difficulty. The Arabic celestial ascension is therefore no miraculous flight: it is a climbing by degrees, an arduous scaling, an obstinate march toward what refuses itself — at the price of a fatigue that may become torment.
ʿ-r-j.meditation: What step can I climb today — small, but real?
forms: to make climb, to make a halt
the ladder of ascension, the nocturnal ascension
quran: From God, Master of the degrees. The angels and the Breath climb toward Him.
Glory to the One who made His servant journey by night. (the verse of the night journey / miʿrāj)

q-w-l.core_fr: The word — to say, to declare, to make be by the voice
q-w-l.field: To say. Quranic cosmology: God creates by saying kun (Be) and fa-yakūn (it is). The divine qawl is performative — it does not describe, it brings into being. The human qawl is called to imitate, in its measure, this dignity.
q-w-l.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root the broadest possible definition of speech: to pronounce (a word, a discourse), to speak, to utter, to address speech to someone, to have a large mouth, to communicate by the voice. The noun qawl is word, saying, statement, declaration, utterance. And — a capital detail — the same root yields the imitative verb qāla (“he said”), the most frequent in the Quran. For Gloton, the Quranic qawl encompasses all that comes from the mouth: the true word, the empty word, slander — hence the particular responsibility of the tongue in classical Sufism.
q-w-l.meditation: My word, this morning — is it sadīda: upright, just, effective for the good?
forms: the one who says, the speaker
Say! (a frequent imperative opening a sūra)
quran: O you who believe, be vigilant toward God, and speak an upright word.
When He decrees a thing, He simply says to it: Be — and it is.

s-ʾ-l.core_fr: The question — to ask, to beg, to inquire
s-ʾ-l.field: To ask: to put a question, to solicit, to beg. On the Day, each one will be masʾūl (questioned). Prayer is a suʾāl — of which the Prophet said: prayer is the marrow of worship. To ask is not weakness: it is the right posture of the created.
s-ʾ-l.glose_gloton: Gloton gives for this root an apparently simple definition: to ask, to question, to inquire of someone. But the unfolding of the noun is rich: question, request, petition, prayer, supplication, begging. For Gloton, the root s-ʾ-l encompasses the whole range of asking — from the intellectual question to begging. And the Quran insists: “Ask those who know” (16:43). The question is no mark of ignorance — it is a gateway to knowledge. And spiritual begging (to be a sāʾil before God) is the very condition of the created being: “O humankind, it is you who are the beggars in need of God” (35:15).
s-ʾ-l.meditation: What question, within me, asks to be put — not to another, but to Him?
forms: the one who asks, the beggar
the one called to account, the responsible one
the question (of fiqh or of doctrine)
quran: And you will surely be questioned about what you used to do.
And when My servants ask you about Me — I am near, I answer the call of the one who calls Me.
